title: Operator transformation for regular expressions
description:
### What happens?

Expected (from examples in https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/functions-matching.html#FUNCTIONS-POSIX-REGEXP):

```
'abcd' ~ 'bc'     true
'abcd' ~ 'a.c'    true — dot matches any character
'abcd' ~ 'a.*d'   true — * repeats the preceding pattern item
'abcd' ~ '(b|x)'  true — | means OR, parentheses group
'abcd' ~ '^a'     true — ^ anchors to start of string
'abcd' ~ '^(b|c)' false — would match except for anchoring
```

Currently:

```
select 'abcd' ~ 'bc'; false
select 'abcd' ~ 'a.c'; false
select 'abcd' ~ 'a.*d'; true
select 'abcd' ~ '(b|x)'; false
select 'abcd' ~ '^a'; false
select 'abcd' ~ '^(b|c)'; false
```

### To Reproduce

```
select 'abcd' ~ 'bc'; false
select 'abcd' ~ 'a.c'; false
select 'abcd' ~ 'a.*d'; true
select 'abcd' ~ '(b|x)'; false
select 'abcd' ~ '^a'; false
select 'abcd' ~ '^(b|c)'; false
```

From my understanding of the postgres documentation:

`SIMILAR TO` is similar to regexp_full_match(), `~` (posix operator) is similar to regexp_matches().

### OS:

Linux

### DuckDB Version:

v1.2.1 8e52ec4395

### DuckDB Client:

bug_features_seed: CREATE TABLE test_regex (id INT PRIMARY KEY, pattern VARCHAR(255), test_string VARCHAR(255));
INSERT INTO test_regex (id, pattern, test_string) VALUES (1, 'bc', 'abcd'), (2, 'a.c', 'abcd'), (3, 'a.%d', 'abcd'), (4, '(b|x)', 'abcd'), (5, '^a', 'abcd'), (6, '^(b|c)', 'abcd');
SELECT id, test_string REGEXP pattern AS match_result FROM test_regex;
SELECT id, test_string REGEXP 'a.%d' AS match_result FROM test_regex WHERE pattern = 'a.%d';
SELECT id, test_string REGEXP '^(b|c)' AS match_result FROM test_regex WHERE test_string IS NOT NULL;
key_bug_features: Incorrect regex operator behavior for pattern matching
